author: @hunnybunnysheavymetalmusic6542
text:
Those little bellows look like they would work well for a low pressure water pump.

Be it a swaying or rotating wind driver, one could put one of those at the bottom of a small body of water and hook it to something to move it, say in a shallow pool in a stream where a much larger pump would not fit, and it could move enough water to survive on, if the wind or water flow nearby were sufficient.

A tiny water wheel or little wind vane, and this would make a neat portable survival device where water was shallow and some place where digging a pit were not practical [like against solid bedrock].

author: @josephpk4878
text: There's some good filament comparison test videos on YT. The results of PLA are actually quite impressive, when compared to the alternatives - ABS, CF, PETG, etc. Good tensile strength, flexibility, durability... and your shop doesn't smell like an electrical fire is about to happen (ABS reminds me of that alarming odour). If you set up your printer perfectly, then your PLA results will be good enough for 90% of your hard plastic applications... in my opinion.

text: PETG is also UV resistant, higher heat resistant(can print stuff for your car interior that won't melt in the sun), just to add to every one else's praise of the material. Fun stuff, never stop learning!

author: @lagunafishing
text: ABS can be tricky and often results in blocked nozzles and ruined prints. One option to increase surface finish and durability of PLA parts is to coat them in an ABS slurry made by dissolving ABS in a solvent which can be dipped or painted on. Obviously you would need to ensure adequate ventilation and not breathe in the fumes, but the process does produce superior surface finishes and improves impact resistance of parts.

author: @PetraKann
text:
PLA is a type of polyester made from fermented plant starch from corn, cassava, maize, sugarcane or sugar beet pulp. The sugar in these renewable materials are fermented and turned into lactic acid, when is then made into polylactic acid, or PLA.

You can also make PLA from milk: although much more expensive

author: @PaulLemars01
text: Our printer lives in the family room in our small apartment. PETG is my go-to filament for pretty much everything these days. It's food safe and as strong as ABS but no stink when printing. The only issue is gluing, Cyanoacrylate doesn't really work but foaming polymer glue such as Gorilla Glue works perfectly. It's a bugger to paint, PLA is much better. It's about the same hardness as PLA but it can flex more.  PETG is the same plastic as all those soda (PET) and other similar bottles plus a bit of glycerin (G) to lower the melt temperature. You can actually print the original PET quite easily but it requires an all-metal hot end. PETG is a bit of a sticky filament and I've never really pushed it beyond 60 mm/s. I actually have two unopened rolls of ABS that I'm sending off to the thrift store because I'll never use them. The other benefit for PETG here in the States is that it's dirt cheap. Most brands are well under $20 a kilo. I just bought two 1Kg rolls of black Sunlu PETG for $14 each off Amazon shipping included.

author: @robertsmith4681
text: I personally have never had a use for filaments other than "PLA plus", have yet to see any visible degradation of old prints made with that stuff. The "biodegradable" aspect of it in my experience is borderline false advertisement, seen 'experiments" done which consisted in burying some parts in somebody's yard for up to a year and when dug up and washed off there was not a hint of it having degraded in any visible way.

author: @mordiev
text: One more useful tip for your 3D printing.  Most likely you are using the most common 0.4 mm nozzle.  Try using a 0.6 mm nozzle instead.  You will lose a small amount of detail, but you will have faster and stronger prints.

replies.author: @robertsmith4681
replies.text: I've considered switch to nozzles as 'large" as a full 1mm for both speed of printing and what I would assume would be more relative strength of the printer parts but have yet to tinker with this sort of thing.

author: @mordiev
text: Love the channel.  I was really happy to see you adopt 3d printing.  I have printed over 8,000 objects over the years.  Over 6,000 on FDM printers.  PETG is my go to filament for 95% of my prints now.  It prints almost as easily as PLA. Its shrinkage is similar to PLA.  It's a little more flexible than PLA.  It doesn't stink or warp like ABS.  PETG can be used outside because it won't melt in sunlight like PLA.  Its melting point is higher than PLA but lower than ABS.  It does like to be printed in a draft free environment.  Moving air can cause it to warp or peel up from the bed.  Even a tent/curtain around the printer will work.  You may also need to change your print bed to use a PEI powder coated (aka textured) surface. Smooth PEI either sticks too well and takes damage or not well enough and the print falls off mid print.  It does emit a white powder dust when it prints.  If you are using an enclosure (even a non vented one) the dust will collect harmlessly inside and you can wipe it away with a rag once in a while.  It is best to find a quality brand and stick with it as cheap brands are terrible to work with.  For you, I would suggest you keep using PLA for prototypes but when you want to make something more durable then reprint it using PETG instead.  For the parts that need to be really durable to go with Nylon.  Keep up the great work.

replies.author: @LawrenceMarks27
replies.text: Yea - I use PETG nearly exclusively as well.  PLA is just too brittle.  And frankly, lately I've been finding it easier to print with PETG than PLA.

author: @WTF_BBQ
text: I use PETG. They are easy to print like PLA, but superior in mechanical performance and has more resistance to heat. The only downside is that the print speed is similar to TPU.  I've been interested in nylon lately for parts that require extreme durability. I tried it before and it didn't turn out well. It seems they absorb water faster than other filaments even while printing.
